coredata.dc:title: Thiol-functionalized single-layered MoS2 nanosheet as a photoluminescence sensing platform via charge transfer for dopamine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Bobin
Su, Yingying
Li, Li
Liu, Rui
Lv, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Novel fluorescent thioglycollic acid (TGA) surface-functionalized single-layered thickness structured MoS2 nanosheets were purposely synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment of using molybdenyl acetylacetonate, TGA and sodium sulfide as precursors. During the process of synthesis, we subtly solved the problem of the poor water solubility of the precursor molybdenyl acetylacetonate, and obtained thiolated single-layer MoS2 nanosheet (TGA-MoS2) with excellent water solubility and stable fluorescence property. The fluorescence of TGA-modified MoS2 nanosheets was significantly suppressed in the presence of dopamine (DA), and a simple sensing system for highly selective, fast, direct detection of dopamine was established. Meanwhile, the present methodology has a wide detection range of 0.05μΜ to 20μΜ, a comparable detection limit of 27nM and an extremely short response time of 3min. To understand the fast response behavior, we tried effort to explain the interaction mechanisms between TGA-MoS2 nanosheets and the quencher DA. Further research suggests that the possible mechanism might be the charge transfer (CT) by the stacking of MoS2 layers through CO⋯HO hydrogen boding interaction, providing a fast fluorescence sensing platform for DA determination in urine and serum sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2 nanosheet
Fluorescence
Dopamine
Single-layered
Functionality

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of dopamine (DA) in real samples
Fluorescence properties and optimization of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets
The dopamine detection and selectivity performance of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets-based sensing platform
The application of the proposed method in real samples assay
Possible mechanism of the sensing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM image of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets deposited on a mica substrate and the corresponding height images.
(A) XRD pattern of the TGA-MoS2 nanosheets. (B) and(C) TEM images of the TGA-MoS2 nanosheets. (D) HRTEM images of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets.
(A) UV–vis spectroscopy of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets (B)-(D) XPS spectra of C 1s, Mo 3d, S 2p of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets.

(A) The Raman spectra of MoS2 bulk reference (black line) and TGA-MoS2 nanosheets (red line). (B) The Raman spectra of TGA molecules and TGA-MoS2 nanosheets. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Emission spectra of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets at excitation wavelengths progressively increasing from 270 to 345nm. (B) Excitation spectra (dashed line) and emission spectra (solid line) of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets (λ excitation=315nm, λ emission=420nm) in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 16μΜ of dopamine (DA) (C) Day-to-day fluorescence stability and (D) Effect of pH on the PL intensity of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets(inset picture)Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets upon addition of 16μM DA at wavelength scan program.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets-based sensor toward various concentrations of dopamine (DA): 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20μM from top to bottom. Standard curve (equation:I 0 − I =300.4C +148.6 ; R =0.998) for the determination of dopamine (DA) concentration. (B) Fluorescence response of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets in PBS (pH 11.0, 5mM) with different guest molecules. The concentrations of all analytes were 10μM except for TBC and catechol (15μM).

(A) UV–vis spectra of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets (black curve) and dopamine (red curve) (B) FT-IR spectra of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets, TGA-MoS2-DA complex and DA molecule (C) AFM images of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets upon addition of 20μΜ of DA, showing the height incensement about 60nm (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of 20μM DA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Analytical results for the determination of dopamine (DA) in serum samples and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Thiol-functionalized single-layered MoS2 nanosheet as a photoluminescence sensing platform via charge transfer for dopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, 610064, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel fluorescent thioglycollic acid (TGA) surface-functionalized single-layered thickness structured MoS2 nanosheets were purposely synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment of using molybdenyl acetylacetonate, TGA and sodium sulfide as precursors. During the process of synthesis, we subtly solved the problem of the poor water solubility of the precursor molybdenyl acetylacetonate, and obtained thiolated single-layer MoS2 nanosheet (TGA-MoS2) with excellent water solubility and stable fluorescence property. The fluorescence of TGA-modified MoS2 nanosheets was significantly suppressed in the presence of dopamine (DA), and a simple sensing system for highly selective, fast, direct detection of dopamine was established. Meanwhile, the present methodology has a wide detection range of 0.05μΜ to 20μΜ, a comparable detection limit of 27nM and an extremely short response time of 3min. To understand the fast response behavior, we tried effort to explain the interaction mechanisms between TGA-MoS2 nanosheets and the quencher DA. Further research suggests that the possible mechanism might be the charge transfer (CT) by the stacking of MoS2 layers through CO⋯HO hydrogen boding interaction, providing a fast fluorescence sensing platform for DA determination in urine and serum sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials have attracted intensive study over the last decade, showing great potential in various applications such as electronic devices, catalysis, energy storage, and sensing [1–6]. Among these various 2D nanomaterials, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets with single or few atomic layers has attracted huge attention in the recent years because of their direct intrinsic bandgap, high mobility, ultrahigh specific surface area and strong quantum confinement of electrons in two dimension. It is composed of hexagonal layers of metal Mo atoms which are sandwiched between two layers of S atoms [4], as shown in Fig. 1 . Unlike graphene, it has a layer-dependent bandgap that changes from indirect (1.29eV) to direct in a single layer (1.9eV) [7]. This transition from indirect bandgap to direct bandgap has been confirmed by strong photoluminescence of single-layer MoS2 [4] , which should be of great use in biosensing applications.

However, compared with the large number of applications in energy storage [8,9] and transistors [10], the use of MoS2 nanosheets as a sensing platform, especially for fluorescence-based biosensing, is still largely under exploration [11]. Fortunately, Kalantar-zadeh and coworkers have conducted some inspiring studies on this area [12–16]. Firstly, they introduced an electrochemical approach to control the photoluminescence (PL) of 2D MoS2 nanoflakes and presented their potential applications in bio-optical sensors [12]. Afterwards, they successfully set up two representative models of biological systems that incorporate the ion-controlled PL of 2D MoS2 nanoflakes, which provides a strong base for the future exploration of MoS2 material in biological and medical field [13]. Meanwhile, in 2013, Zhang and coworkers also developed a single-layer MoS2 nanosheets-based sensing platform for the detection of DNA and small molecules [4]. Subsequently, on the base of this strategy, Ai et al. reported a novel MoS2 nanosheets based micro-fluidic ultrasensitive biosensor [17]. Ge and coworkers reported a fluorescence-activated DNA–MoS2 nanosheets biosensor for single-step detection protein and small molecule targets [18]. Inspired by these limited number of pioneering work [4,17–19], we aim to explore more possibilities in the application of fluorescence-based biosensing of MoS2 nanosheets.

Offering some new property and improving selective recognition, surface-modification of materials has been proved to be an effective way to construct sensitive and selective sensors [20,21]. Although MoS2 material have great potential in surface functionalization [15,16,22,23], the research in this field is still limited. One work is that thionin-modified MoS2 nanosheets can bind strongly to dsDNA through intercalation and electrostatic interaction, thus it was used to construct DNA electrochemical sensors via its own redox decreasing signal when modified on the electrode [24]. In the other work, mercaptoundecaonic acid (MUA) was conjugated to the surface of MoS2 for the fabrication of a highly sensitive, stable, and inexpensive chemi-resistor sensor for VOCs detection [25]. To the best of our knowledge, the reason why there are only few reports on surface-modification of MoS2 nanosheets might be that most studies only focused on its single and or few-layer structure and it is difficult to synthesize modified MoS2 nanosheets with excellent water solubility and stable optical property. Therefore, it is of great necessity to develop a new fabrication method that provides atomic-thick sheet-like structures, a simple process, cost-effectiveness, stable fluorescence, and surface functionalization for certain detection.

Herein, we reported a hydro/solvothermal synthesis for the preparation of thioglycollic acid (TGA) functionalized single-layered thickness structured MoS2 nanosheets by using molybdenyl acetylacetonate, TGA and sodium sulfide as precursors. In this case, the precursor (TGA) was proved to be interacting with the MoS2 nanosheets surface, which significantly increased the water solubility of MoS2 nanosheets by exposing alkyl chains of TGA molecules with end carboxyl group. Besides, we have observed that the fluorescence of TGA-MoS2 can be remarkably quenched after addition of dopamine (DA), a member of phenethylamine family with two hydroxyls (OH) groups and one amino (NH2) group on the benzene ring. [26,27] Based on this phenomenon, the prepared TGA-MoS2 nanosheets were explored as a fluorescent probe for highly selective analysis of DA in human serum and urine samples. Furthermore, the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was studied. It suggests that quenching phenomenon might be attributed to the charge transfer (CT) mechanism by the stacking of MoS2 layers through CO⋯HO hydrogen boding interaction. We hope that the present work would inspire researches to exploit the widespread biological applications of the emerging MoS2 nanomaterials.

All the reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Molybdenyl acetylacetonate (MoO2(ACAC)2) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Thioglycollic acid (TGA), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H20) were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Company (China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), l-ascorbic acid (AA), l-methionine (Met), l-cysteine (Cys), l-tryptophan (Trp), l-serine (Ser) and l-arginine (Arg) were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Company (China). While l-norepinephrine Hydrochloride (NE), 4-tert-Butylcatechol(TBC), catechol, uric acid (UA), l-histidine (His), d,l-homocysteine (Hcy), glycine (Gly), l-Threonine (Thr) and l-glutathione (GSH) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. l-lysine (Lys) and l-tryptophan (Trp) were obtained from Huishi Biochemical Reagent Company (China). Deionized water with conductivity of 18.2MΩcm−1 from a water purification system (ULUPURE, Chengdu, China) was used in this work.

All fluorescence measurements were carried out on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a U-2910 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Philips X’ Pert Pro X-ray diffractometer employing Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å). The fluorescence lifetime was measured on a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France), with NanoLED-350 used as excitation source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL Co., Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Atomic force microscope (AFM) measurements were made on an SPM-9600 Instrument in the tapping mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCA Lab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA) using 200W monochromated Al Kα radiation. The surface charge of the products was performed with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Co., U.K.). Raman spectrum was performed on a Lab Ram HR800 Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France, laser excitation wavelength at 532nm).

TGA-MoS2 nanosheets were synthesized through a hydrothermal method using molybdenyl acetylacetonate, thioglycolic acid and sodium sulfide. In brief, 5mL of 0.01M SHCH2COOH was added into 95mL of deionized water followed by the addition of 0.081g MoO2(ACAC)2 under magnetic stirring (500rmin−1). After heated to 100°C and kept for 1h, the solution was adjusted to pH 11.0 by 0.1M NaOH. Then, 0.5g of Na2S was added to the solution followed by ultra-sonication for 10min. Thereafter, the as-obtained mixture was placed in two 100mL Teflon-lined autoclaves and reacted at 220°C for 20h under static conditions. After the solution cooled naturally, the acquired colorless transparent suspensions were collected after being purified through dialysis (Mw: 500) to remove the extra small organic molecules and redundant irons.

In a typical dopamine sensing experiments, fluorescence experiments were carried out in a 1cm×1cm quartz cell, where 1800μL of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets suspension, 100μL of phosphate buffered solution (PBS, 0.1M), and 100μL of different concentrations dopamine solution were added sequentially and mixed for fluorescence measurements by excitation with 315nm light. The fluorescence spectrum of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets in PBS buffer (5mM, pH 11.0) was collected 15 times to determine the background noise σ. All measurements were taken at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dopamine (DA) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets as a fluorescent probe for detecting DA in serum and urine samples were put into practice. The serum samples were obtained from local hospital and the urine samples were collected from three volunteers. The freshly collected serum and urine samples were centrifuged at the speed of 8000rmin−1 for 20min before the determination to eliminate the interferences from the proteins.

The MoS2 nanosheets were characterized by means of TEM, AFM, XPS, FT-IR, PL, Raman and UV–vis spectroscopy. The diffraction peaks at 13.8, 33, 39, and 59.8° were shown in the patterns (Fig. 2 A) [28,29]. The peak at 13.8° corresponding to the (002) plane of MoS2 is lower than that of bulk MoS2 (2θ=14.4°, d=0.614nm), suggesting an enlarged interlayer distance the (002) plane [30]. This increased interlayer distance can be attributed to its ultrathin structure and the weak interaction between layers [31].

It can be seen from TEM images (Fig. 2B and C) that the MoS2 nanosheets are curled and overlapped as a result of its ultrathin features and the surface modification. Moreover, the distance of the interlayer of the (002) plane is calculated to be 0.64nm (Fig. 2D), which is consistent with the result of XRD using Bragg equation. AFM was used to determine the thickness of the MoS2 nanosheets. The average thickness of MoS2 nanosheets is around 0.93nm (Fig. 1), confirming the production of single-layer nanosheets [31].

From the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3 A), we can see three weak absorption peaks at 340, 605 and 688nm, which was similar to the previously reported result [12]. The two weak absorption peaks at 605 and 688nm can be ascribed to B and A excitonic peaks respectively, which were arise from the K point of the Brillouin zone in 2D MoS2 with large lateral dimensions [4]. Accordingly, it has been suggested that the optical absorption of low-dimensional or quantum dot MoS2 exhibits a strong blue shift when the lateral dimensions of the MoS2 nanostructures are reduced to <50nm, ascribed to the quantum size effect [32]. Therefore, the other shoulder absorption peak at 340nm can be assigned to the direct transition from the deep valence band to the conduction band in MoS2 with smaller size [33,34].

To verify TGA functionalities were interacting on the MoS2 surface through the adopted method, XPS and Raman Spectroscopy analysis were performed. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3B) via peak fitting revealed three peaks at 284.7eV, (CC) 288.5eV, (-COOH) 287.8eV (C-S) [25]. These arise from the TGA molecules and distinctly indicate TGA on the MoS2 nanosheets. The three characteristic peaks (Fig. 3C) emerged at around 226.0eV, 229.8eV, and 231.6eV, corresponded to the S 2s, Mo 3d5/2, and Mo 3d3/2 lines of MoS2 binding energy, respectively [35,36]. Small sub-bands of Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2 at 232.3.1eV and 234.9eV are also observed, suggesting the presence of Mo6+, which might arise from MoO3 due to surface oxidization [37]. The two S 2p doublets at around 162.1 and 163.2eV (Fig. 3D) can be attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 lines of MoS2 [38] The S 2p doublet at around 163.4eV (2p3/2) and 164.1eV (2p1/2) were very likely derived from disulfides S2 2− or polysulfide Sn 2− [38–40].

We performed Raman spectra analysis (Fig. 4 ) to further confirm TGA functionalization on the MoS2 nanosheets. A typical TGA-modified single-layer MoS2 nanosheet (confirmed by AFM) gives bands at 384.7 and 404.5cm−1, which are associated to the
E
2 g
1
(in-plane) and A1g (out-of-plane) phonon modes [41]. The corresponding bands for bulk MoS2 are located at 382.9 and 408.1cm−1. The frequency differences of the two modes at 19.82cm−1 in 1L-MoS2 nanosheet, and 25.23cm−1 in bulk from are consistent with previously reported results [42,43]. Moreover, as is shown in Fig. 4B, the S–H vibration (about 2576cm−1) of the TGA molecule disappeared whereas the characteristic peak for CH2 (about 2933cm−1) still existed [44]. This change further confirmed the interaction between TGA and MoS2 nanosheets. Some researchers claimed that conjugation of TGA could be accomplished because of the higher molecular affinity between the thiol group in TGA and sulfur vacancies on the MoS2 flakes [45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and optimization of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As is shown in Fig. 5 A, the FL emission spectra of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets were plotted at excitation wavelengths progressively increasing from 270 to 345nm. It was clear that when the excitation energy was at the wavelength of 315nm, a strongest peak centered at 420nm (see from Fig. 5B). The phenomenon is similar with the previous literatures reported [12,13]. In their work, the PL peak position of MoS2 nanoflakes is not in 680nm and instead around 450nm. Meanwhile, TGA-MoS2 nanosheets displays excitation wavelength independent PL feature. The effects can be explained by many other previously reported literature [12–14,47,48], which demonstrated that the peak at 420nm can be ascribed to the blue shifted hot PL from the K point.

Otherwise, TGA-MoS2 nanosheets also possess a stable day-to-day fluorescence ability (Fig. 5C) and pH-dependent property in the range of 3.0–12.0 (Fig. 5D). The fluorescence intensity hardly changed in the range of 3.0–6.0 and increased with the value from 6.0 to 11.0. Besides, the pH values of the TGA-MoS2 nanosheets do not change after addition of DA. Therefore, pH 11.0 was chosen as the optimized pH throughout subsequent experiments. Just as seen in the inset of Fig. 5D, the fluorescence intensity of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets rapidly reduced by about 80% of the original value as soon as 16μM of DA is added and further reduced for 3min. To ensure the consistency of experimental conditions, 3min is conducted as the collection time of fluorescence data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dopamine detection and selectivity performance of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets-based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The linear change of fluorescence intensity after addition of DA at different concentrations were measured for quantitative DA detection (Fig. 6 A), which suggests a good linear relationship (R =0.998) between the concentration (C) of dopamine and the quenched fluorescence intensity of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets in the range from 0.05 to 20μM. The obtained fluorescence assay also promise low detection limit (S/N=3) of 0.027μM, which is comparable to those of preciously reported, such as GQDs [49], ZnO QDs [50], C3N4-TYR [51], and CdTe QDs [52], with detection limits of 90nM, 12nM, 30nM and 50nM, respectively.

We studied the possible fluorescence quenching effect of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets caused by other common molecules. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of the TGA-MoS2 nanosheets exhibited a significant decrease by adding DA to the solution, whereas other molecules such as amino acids (including Glu, His, Hcy, Gly, Cys, Trp, Lys, Ser, Arg, Tyr, Thr and GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), Uric Acid (UA), Norepinephrine (NE) and ions (including K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Fe3+) did not cause obvious fluorescence changes. Besides, TBC and catechol at 15.0μM can lightly reduce the fluorescence intensity of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets by ∼17% and ∼12.5%, respectively. However, the two compounds hardly exist in a living system and have little influence on the detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of the proposed method in real samples assay
ce:section.ce:para: The application of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets as a fluorescent probe for detecting DA in serum and urine samples were given in Table 1 , which shows that the analysis result is extremely in accordance with the spiked concentration. And the recoveries of standard addition were found to be in the range of 98%–105%. It proved that the present work for DA detection is favorable for analysis in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Most of DA sensors are based on the energy transfer mechanism from fluorescent material to the oxidation product of DA [53,54]. Unlike the previously reported fluorescence-based DA assay, the proposed TGA-MoS2 nanosheets-based DA sensor possess an extremely short response time of 3min, which is much shorter than those obtained in the previously reported DA assay, such as Si NPs [54], PDA NPs [55], and GO [56] with response time of 3h, 40min, and 15min. The fast response behavior has inspired us to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism. We first investigate UV–vis spectrum of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets in the presence of DA, (Fig. 7 A) from which we can see a new absorption peak at 416nm compared with that of DA. Besides DA, the quenching effects of amino acids and phenols on TGA-MoS2 nanosheets were also examined. We found that all the amino acids with NH2 and COO group have no quenching influence on the fluorescence of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets, while phenols with two adjacent OH group, such as TBC (p-tert-Butylcatechol) and catechol, can effectively quench the fluorescence of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets. More importantly, the three different quencher can lead to different quenching quality(DA>TBC>catechol), which was largely depended on the electron withdrawal power of the substituent groups for each quencher on the benzene ring(H >C(CH3)3>NH2). Subsequently, we observed FT-IR spectra (Fig. 7B) and the AFM images (Fig. 7C) of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets upon addition of DA. The FT-IR spectra of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets, TGA-MoS2-DA complex and DA molecule are shown in Fig. 7B, from which it can be seen that the band at 1452cm−1 for OH vibration stretching of diols in TGA-MoS2-DA complex are much weaker and 47cm−1 shifted compared with that of diols in DA. In addition, the band for the stretching vibration of CO in the TGA-MoS2-DA complex gave a slightly higher frequency (1633cm−1) than that in TGA-MoS2 nanosheets mainly because of the strong multiple noncovalent interactions between DA and TGA-MoS2. All the results confirmed that the interaction between DA and COO group on TGA-MoS2 nanosheets are due to the hydrogen-bonding interaction of the diols in DA with TGA-MoS2 nanosheets. The topographical changes of TGA-MoS2 upon its interaction with DA were revealed by AFM images (Fig. 7C). Addition of DA induced obvious aggregation via the binder of DA between layers in TGA-MoS2 nanosheets and a significant topographical change along with a height increment of approximately 60nm compared to a single TGA-MoS2 nanosheet (Fig. 2). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets is shortened from 6.1 to 3.7ns after the addition of 10μΜ dopamine (Fig. 7D). During the quenching process, as previously reported, surface functionalization offers an elegant way to control the charge carrier density of MoS2 nanomaterial [16]. Moreover, its photoluminescence might be altered by the interaction of excitons with charge carriers [12]. Based on the above experiments, we believed that the fluorescence quenching was mainly caused by hydrogen bond upon DA attachment to TGA-MoS2 nanosheets via charge transfer mechanism [57,58].

The possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching by DA was shown in Scheme 1 . It is thought that DA molecule worked as a binding bridge between layers in TGA-MoS2 nanosheets, which shortened the distance between TGA-MoS2 nanosheets and amine groups to build CO⋯HO hydrogen bonding. Moreover, the CO⋯HO hydrogen bonding between TGA-MoS2 layers caused the height increment of approximately 60nm, thus the layer-dependent band-gap sharply decreased, resulting in fluorescence quenching.

In summary, single-layered TGA-MoS2 nanosheets with fluorescent stability and water solubility was successfully synthesized. The research suggests that the purposely modified TGA molecule on MoS2 nanosheets surface might be responsible for the quenching process of DA determination. Upon addition of DA, the single-layered TGA-MoS2 nanosheets started to stack layer by layer through CO⋯HO hydrogen boding between the layers, thus quenched the fluorescence of TGA-MoS2 nanosheets by charge transfer mechanism. Unlike the traditional energy transfer based DA fluorescent sensors, ours invalidates the interference from AA, UA, UE and other amino acids and possess a very fast response time of 3mins, paving the way for DA detection in the human urine and serum sample monitoring. More importantly, it explores the widespread biological applications of the emerging MoS2 materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 21375089 and 21505095) and Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province of China (Grant 2015JY0272).

Strong quantum-size effects in a layered semiconductor: MoS2 nanoclusters
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of inorganic and organometallic compounds of molybdenum
Anisotropic oxidation of MoS2 crystallites studied by angle-resolved X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy
Charge transfer in the MoS2/Carbon nanotube composite
Electron spectroscopy for chemical analysis studies of lead sulfide oxidation
Raman study and lattice dynamics of single molecular layers of MoS2
Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots as a photoluminescence sensing platform for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection
Anomalous lattice vibrations of single- and few-layer MoS2
Quantitative Raman spectrum and reliable thickness identification for atomic layers on insulating substrates
A multidimensional sensing device for the discrimination of proteins based on manganese-doped ZnS quantum dots
Ligand conjugation of chemically exfoliated MoS2
Selective adsorption of thiol molecules at sulfur vacancies on MoS2(0001), followed by vacancy repair via S–C dissociation
Strongly luminescent monolayered MoS2 prepared by effective ultrasound exfoliation
Demonstration of photoluminescence and metal-enhanced fluorescence of exfoliated MoS2
Observation of pH- solvent-, spin-, and excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence from carbon nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots as effective probes for label-free fluorescence detection of dopamine
Luminescent ZnO quantum dots for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine
A practical and highly sensitive C3N4-TYR fluorescent probe for convenient detection of dopamine
Enzyme-encapsulating quantum dot hydrogels and xerogels as biosensors: multifunctional platforms for both biocatalysis and fluorescent probing
A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe
Highly sensitive and selective detection of dopamine using one-Pot synthesized highly photoluminescent silicon nanoparticles
Turn-on fluorescent dopamine sensing based on in situ formation of visible light emitting polydopamine nanoparticles
Graphene oxide based photoinduced charge transfer label-free near-infrared fluorescent biosensor for dopamine
Fluorescence turn-on metal ion sensors based on switching of intramolecular charge transfer of donor-acceptor systems
A fluorescent probe for TNP detection in aqueous solution based on joint properties of intramolecular charge transfer and aggregation-induced enhanced emission

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bobin Xu received her B.S. degree in 2014 from Shandong University, China. Now she is a Master candidate of analytical chemistry at Sichuan University. Her research interests include synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in chemical sensor.

Yingying Su received her Ph.D. from Sichuan University in 2007. Now she works at Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, China. Her areas of interest are separation technique, chemiluminescence and luminescence-based sensor.

Li Li received his B.S. degree in 2013 from Le Shan Normal University, China. Now he is a Master candidate of analytical chemistry at Sichuan University. His research interests are focused on synthesis of nanocomposite and their application in chemical sensor.

Rui Liu received his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from the College of Chemistry at Sichuan University in 2012 under the supervision of Prof. Yi Lv. Now he is an assistant professor in the College of Chemistry, Sichuan University. His scientific interests focus on bio-analysis based on spectrometry. He is the author or co-author of 20 publications in peer-reviewed scientific journals.

Yi Lv received his bachelor’s degree, master’s degree and Ph.D. degree from Southwest China Normal University (the current name is Southwest University) in 1997, 2000 and 2003, respectively. After a two-year stay in Tsinghua University as a postdoctoral fellow, he joined the faculty of the College of Chemistry at Sichuan University in 2005. His research interests are mainly in the areas of nanomaterials for analytical chemistry and luminescence-based sensors. He has published over 100 papers on the journals of analytical chemistry and one book chapter on the topics.
